top_rant.text:
First off I am not your regular jacket user. I will get into that in a minute.
Next a few observations about this jacket :
* Over all it is quite well made.

* My boss who NEVER ever hands out a complement or voices much of an opinion on anything good . . . said on first laying eyes on my all black one and I quote "NICE JACKET" !.

* There is no second zipper pull to zip up from the bottom; handy if you want to cinch up a tool belt a notch tighter etc.

* The sleeves are long enough for the size; many times I find jacket sleeves a bit short for really active use.

* THE SLEEVES ARE LINED WITH SLICKish fabric; not the micro fleece. Most people will want this for easy on off over other clothes (I was hoping for micro fleece in the sleeves but as I said I am abnormal : ).

* The sleeves have a thin but very effective layer of synthetic insulation in the arms. Just the right amount for street wear; maybe too much (preferably none) for bicycle commuting.

* The hood is more than an after thought; it is double layer and well made. Works well for street use. Comes forward too much for cycling (I use a pro neoprene balaclava with on off nose cover).

* The cord locks on the stings on the hood and bottom of the jacket are nearly indecipherable but work once you accidentally get one to work. I am very mechanically inclined (it is how I make my living) and they are a complete and utter mystery to me : ).

* There are no under arm zips; I would have paid significantly more for these.

Now to the nitty gritty . . . hang on it is going to be a scary ride !

I commute to work by bicycle all year round in Colorado. yah people try to get me to quit but I am addicted : ).
This means bellow zero temps several times a year, high wind, bright sun( which you wouldn't think is a problem ) which in actuality causes the most problem as far as layering for changing conditions.

I bought this jacket for a quick grab and go jacket at work when I have to make a quick trip out from the over heated indoors to teens or bellow zero outdoors. The hood was a must. Fleece in the sleeves would have been best because I wear a short sleeve work shirt indoors.

I decided to test out the jacket for warmth and overall function by wearing it on my bike commute in the dead of winter. I must disagree with the people who say this jacket is not WARM.
It has the capability of protecting you and keeping you very warm indeed. The key is an athlete's metabolism (meaning a person who jogs a couple of times a week or walks up some steep hills for a significant period of time (((or rides their bike to work now and then))).

I used the jacket day and night in the teens F° in significant winds with just the short sleeve work shirt, this jacket and appropriate hand and head protection and was toasty warm. It was cloudy or dark; if the sun had come out strong on those days and I was going up a hill of any length it would have probably been too warm and I would have wished for under arm zips.

In addition I used the jacket at zero or there abouts in fairly high wind 15 to 25 mph plus my forward speed, I was wearing a light fleece zip up and the short sleeve work shirt and was almost too warm. The wind protection of this jacket is first rate ! ! ! ; high collar, wide storm flap over the zipper, long enough sleeves, velcro adjusters at the cuffs, wind tight fabric (maybe too wind proof for my use I would prefer just a bit more ventilation for uphill with no wind.

For work the jacket has been perfect, quick on and off, didn't even feel the high wind and single digits when I used it last night for a quick twenty minutes to work out side.

I will post if I have any seam failures etc but so far this is a very good product.

I am considering buying the kaki colored one so it isn't too hot in the intense sun we can have here and ripping out the lining and arm insulation because in the thirties and forties this jacket would be too warm for bicycle commuting; a comparable jacket from the cycling apparel companies can easily be $120 to $170 and I need a new cycle commuter jacket. The lack of under arm zips may be the deal breaker though. I haven't decided.

For street use this is a first rate jacket BUY IT !

Update Jan. 2015
I bought the stone color. It is as pictured.
I bought it for riding my bike four miles one way to work. It is too warm to use at all above thirty or thirty five degrees. The light color should help reflect the sun and keep me from getting too hot on those "warm" days.
For cycling I have absolutely no use for the hood, ever, and because it bunches in the collar too much while cycling in a helmet I cut the hood off near where it is sewn in.
A big discovery . . . the fabric is coated. Seriously coated like with rubber. Totally non breathable. Holding up the outer layer only of the hood to a bright light I could see no pinholes of light. I pricked some holes in it to verify this and I could easily see micro pin holes of light where I had poked it.
What does this mean ? Who cares ?

It means the wind proofness is as good as you are going to get. In fact in use it is like wearing a space suit. In zero temp and high, 20 plus mile per hour wind, I feel no chill any where.
If you want water resistant this is it baby. The seams are not taped and sealed like a hard core rain jacket. I am glad of that. At least there is some chance of excess moisture vapor escaping there.
If you are very active, hiking, cycling, working strenuously you are going to turn this jacket into a mini hot house. Get enough moisture build up and it is going to turn to ice next to the fabric. Not sure if that is all that bad since the jacket is so darn warm as long as you are moving. It will have trouble getting dry enough before you wear it again though.

Washing without a dryer to dry it could be a problem. It is going to take for ever, days, for it to dry. I just machine washed mine, (I do not have a drier) turned it wrong side out and put on a hanger above a heater vent and in a day it is almost dry. Without turning wrong side out and wrinkling it up some plan on two days to dry.

Being a very active user of this jacket (cycling some times in harsh sun on very cold days) I may take [[ASIN:B000X4Q4L2 this perforator]] to mine to get some ventilation going.
I don't give a hang about water resistance and wish very strongly that the fabric was totally un coated so it could breath fully. By the way I have had three hundred dollar genuine Gortex wind/rain jackets with zero insulation in them from very well respected out door clothing makers that were just like wearing a plastic bag. I washed them often and still they would not breath well enough to keep me comfortable. And when it rained they soaked through and my skin was wet. That is the main reason I gave up on such over priced crap and started looking at this jacket. I am not a free perspirer I just don't like all that trapped heat and dampness. Any way if you need more ventilation as I do consider getting western with the perforator.

My motto is, in this day and age, what ever you buy is just a kit / blank and you got to take it home and make what you want out of it.

IZOD : If you would make a jacket like this, or a cycling specific jacket (shorter in front, even longer sleeves, longer in back, close fitting similar to this jacket, no insulation or fleece) OUT OF 60 / 40 CLOTH like the old mountain parkas (perfect cloth for very active people) then I would buy at least one maybe two. Double the price and I would still buy them. Pit zips . . . don't forget the pit zips. Got it ? Good !

top_rant.text:
Update: January 2022!

Why aren't these pajamas made anymore? I am *still* wearing them, even after an over 50 pound weight gain and at least 3 sizes larger than when I bought these in 2014. I have definitely gotten my money's worth from the best pajamas ever.

Original:

This review is for: Womens Pajama Set (Tranquility); Texere Bamboo Viscose Eco Friendly Gift Apparel (Apparel) in "Sky" color. [Please be sure to look at the EDIT and UPDATES of this review, located at the bottom.]

I do love the pajamas so far, but I am deducting a star for a very important reason: The tag on the pajamas (the shirt) says 70% bamboo viscose, 25% cotton, 5% spandex. The product detail at the very start says: "95% Bamboo Viscose / 5% Spandex (250 gsm)." The high bamboo content was a buying point for me, thus I have to deduct a star--even though I love them.

The cotton is what makes this fabric a bit heavier than a higher content of bamboo, and takes longer to dry. Don't get me wrong, these are so comfortable when I tried them on I didn't even want to take them off to wash them first, which I always do to new clothes--sensitive skin! I was surprised at how long it took to dry these and kept wondering why it was taking so long. When I looked at the tag, and saw the cotton content being 25%, I knew my answer. This important information may be a deal breaker for some people, so know this in advance! I intend to contact the seller to let them know about this discrepancy.

That said, I love that the bottoms have pockets. To my delight, these pajamas (XL, the blue set) run large--my personal preference. They fit me like a 1X or 2X would at my current weight of about 200 lbs (I'm 5' 2 3/4" in height). This makes for a very comfortable fit for me-- it's not binding, nor does it seem like it will fall off while walking around. I have extreme weight/mass fluctuations (seriously, I can change a full bra size overnight--I'm not joking; I didn't know that was possible!); so when I "swell" (like edema, but my whole body), these will accommodate the swelling and unswelling. The legs are long on me, as I anticipated because I'm short-- I'm used to that and it's not a deal-breaker. The draw string on the bottoms is narrow, as is the elastic within the band-- something I prefer in nighttime wear. I'm very large busted, broad shoulders, and have "flabby" upper arms-- the top fit very comfortably, no binding or constriction (which is what I typically find on women's clothing).

The fabric seems to be a very good quality, seams are nicely done. There's a convenient seamed "slit" (about 3" long) on the bottom sides of the top, allowing for easy access to the bottom's pockets! As previously stated, I washed them in my front loader on "fast wash" with some of my other new items (some bamboo, cotton, rayon mixes)-- I haven't noticed any bleeding from the initial wash, the color is still vibrant. I started drying it in my heavy duty dryer on low, as recommended for it (and all the other items, except the line dry stuff) for a 30 minute cycle. Most of the other clothes were dry by that time, but these were not. I ran it again on med/low for 15 minutes, still not totally dry. I hung them up for a bit, thinking maybe the "coolness" (which I felt when I tried them on) was just the softness of the fabric (I have a difficult time being able to sense the difference between "cold" and "wet" material). My teen daughter felt them and confirmed they were still damp around the pockets and along the hem lines. I put the set back in on med for 15 minutes and that finished getting them dry (but too late to wear tonight, as I'd been looking forward to doing!). That's when I checked the tag and saw the cotton content.

I love wearing cotton, bamboo and rayon fabrics. I have sensitive skin and health issues that make these materials my preferred choice for next-to-the-skin wear. The cotton content of these pajamas is fine with me, though unexpected. I think that having the cotton will actually help the pajamas last longer (laundering & general wear and tear), than if it were the higher bamboo content.

I recommend these pajamas--with the important notice at the beginning of this review-- for anyone who loves soft fabric and easy wearing. Do note the cotton makes these pajamas feel heavier than would a 95% bamboo/5%spandex blend! I also recommend these for women like me who suffer weight fluctuations, curvaceousness, and medical issues making pajamas the primary clothes for wearing.

I definitely would purchase more of these (I'd love to have them in all the colors, and maybe 2 of each set-- I LOVE wearing pajamas!) at a below $55 range. I would not normally spend even $50 on a pajama set, but these are worth that!

If anything changes with these pajamas after wearing them for a while, I will amend this review. In the meanwhile, I'm adding a set to my wish list! :-)
~CS
[edit]p.s.
I contacted Amazon customer service and they have gotten back to me already; they will try to let the seller know about the inaccurate fabric content listing. :-)
p.p.s.
I'm wearing the pajamas right now. . . definitely the most comfy I've worn! The brilliancy & vividness of the blue color makes me smile. The lace on the top isn't nearly irritating to my skin as I thought it would me. Right now, I'm thinking I could easily spend a week in these! Love them.

**UPDATE**
28 Mar 2015
I still can't add the 5th star because of the content list, however, I LOVE LOVE LOVE these pajamas-- the "tranquility" style. Right now I am wearing the black set. I have ordered each of the "tranquility" style colors (two in the black; one L and one XL) and they are--by far-- my favorite pajamas to wear. I recently recommended them to a friend (about the same time as ordering my black sets *wink*).
Of all the styles--and I've tried a few of them by TexereSilk-- these "Tranquility" fit me best. I'm short, very curvacious, broad shoulders, big-diameter arms, heavily breasted, and very big thighs; I can comfortably wear the L and XL.
I cannot recommend these enough! They are awesome!

EDIT: 20 July 2016
I double-checked the description and the seller has corrected the fabric content; I have given the 5th star (from 4).
I would also like people to know these pajamas have lasted and STILL FIT even after I rapidly put on over 40 pounds because of a medication needed to save my life (a corticosteroid, which also happens to make for "belly fat," which I hadn't had before). From 2014 to May 2016 I was on the corticosteroid, and being so very, very sick in 2015, I basically "lived" in the Tranquility line of pajamas-- I'm talking a couple of days at a time before I could even shower. The pajamas held up not only to the rapid weight gain, but were the only thing comfortable for me to wear while I was so sick. They have held up to laundering very well, kept their vibrant coloring, and are just as comfortable as ever. These are pretty much the best pajamas _ever_. I may order a couple more, still in the XL, because I still have a long (as in several months) recovery to go. Even at my max weight that I hit (240 lbs!), I could still get the Large size on, but the band of the pants was too tight for comfort. The XLs are fantastic-- they aren't "binding" at all, and I'm only down to 233-235 so far. Pockets in the pants, vibrant colors, great comfort, and darn good looking! I seriously LOVE these pajamas!

UPDATE: March 2017
Guess what? I still LOVE these pajamas! They have gone with me through weight fluctuations, mass fluctuations (including the fluctuations occurring at different parts of my body), sickness and health! hehehehe I believe these are the best pajamas I've ever had, and I cannot recommend them enough! "The only tough choice is which color to wear!" :-D

Major bonus points to the lack of "shrinkage" with the Tranquility line of pajamas by Texere! I have gone through other pajamas, in less time, that I ended up having to donate or toss because they shrunk so much (usually the sleeves get to be too short, and I do NOT have long arms!). These pajamas (along with the colors I own in black and purple) have held up through laundering with no problems. The black still look new, the blue (which I have worn the most) look almost new (some coffee stains?), the Amethyst is only slightly faded (likely due to the Borax during laundering). All the seams are still as they should be, no holes or tears, fabric is still thick and no pilling, "lace" part is not scratchy. Pockets are still in perfect shape (and they're big enough for a decent-sized cell phone to fit with no worry about it falling out); oh, and the draw strings! No fraying of the drawstring. These pajamas launder fantastically! I wear these pajamas (in the Sky, the black, and the Amethyst) more than any other. Yes, I very highly recommend these pajamas. I am certain they will still last me for many more years to come!

top_rant.text:
I am very happy with this little "Fleiger style" watch, particularly with it's dial, which compares to any that I have seen in the affordable watch space. The dial has a great deal of well composed detail, this is subtle and hard to see in photographs. In person this very inexpensive watch actually looks high-end.

It is indeed a small watch, 36.5 mm; be aware of that. It's cute as a button, and not much bigger. I have to adjust a bit after wearing this watch to a feeling that my other watches are clownishly large.

Of the 5 different models in the SNK80x series, I recommend this one. This model has a different "color scheme", not just a different colored dial. Notice the black outlining of the handset (the others all have monochromatic handsets). The slender ring between the edge and center of the watch, providing separation between the hours and minute numerals, is gold on this model, but black or white on the other models. The lighter dial provides a background that allows similar adjacent colors of the silver Seiko logo and golden '5" numeral to appear more distinctly, and this cream- light-beige tone also nicely complements the pale green color of the 'super-luminova' application around the dial and on the handset. These, and other little subtleties of the color scheme implemented for the SNK803, add sophistication to the dial that I don't find in the other models. The choice between the models is subjective, of course, and if only black will do, then by all means, get the black version. But the 803 model makes use of color in a way that adds refinement, and puts it over the top for me!

Note: many photographs of this model make it appear that the color is a darker, brownish yellow. The dial doesn't really shift in color as such photos suggest. The first photo of the listing is pretty close to the comparatively light, creamy-beige, actual color.

I like the stainless steel case. It has an interesting multi-layered construction, with the top part having the bead-blasted finish and the bottom (as well as the sides between the lugs) being highly polished. The round bezel formed out of the top section simulates a third layer. The case's sleek lines and tiered geometry contribute significantly to the SNK803's visual appeal.

This watch has Seiko "Lumi-Bright" (sic) applied to small pips around the dial, and on the hands. I think it's the same compound Seiko uses on other models; it's quickly responsive to charging by a light source, and glows very brightly at first. The initial light-show is very pretty. However, not much surface area is allotted for lume on this watch, so the brightness seems to fade a little faster than on models with more copious amounts of lume, such as Sieko's dive watches.

This is a "Seiko 5" watch. "5" is actually a specification for 5 features - i. automatic movement, ii. Day, iii.Date, iv shock resistance and v. water resistance. The basic idea is that Seiko 5 Sports models should be rugged and suitable for outdoor activities. The low cost of these SNK80x models makes them attractive as 'beater' watches, and I've watched reviews on Youtube from people who who attest to the outstanding durability of this watch, after having worn them for years while working in physically demanding, outdoorsy professions

However, water resistance on this model may be a little questionable. It's specified as "water resistant" on the case-back. This is the most minimal water resistance level, just above "not water resistant at all". Seiko is pretty good about testing and actually meeting their specifications, including those for water resistance, but I've heard some reports specific to this model that suggest it may not represent Seiko's best (as far as making the case water resistant).. It's not designed for extended submersion under water, etc. Also - the canvas strap doesn't seem very compatible with aquatic adventure; seems likely to get mildew.
...speaking of the strap, I have trouble understanding all the bitching and moaning about it in other reviews. I think it's a pretty good strap, It looks good with the watch. But I haven't had the watch long enough to know how it holds up or whether it becomes uncomfortable.
Update: I still think the strap is under-rated, but I eventually changed it out in favor of a Seiko OEM bracelet from the black version (SNK809?) which is the only model that is available with a metal bracelet - it's bead blasted and nicely matches the case).

This watch contains an "un-adjusted" Seiko 7S26 movement. This movement's strong point is that it continues to run reliably, just about as accurately, for a very long time before requiring service - typically well over 12 years. Up to 20+ is quite possible. Some people claim to have Seiko watches from the 60s (with very similar movement inside) that are still running beautifully without ever visiting a watch maker (50+ years!).

Note: mine was +20 secs/day, out of the box, not good, the worst Seiko I've ever seen. So I removed the case-back and adjusted the watch's timing by using a tooth-pick to move the regulation lever the slightest amount possible towards "-". I wasn't even sure I had moved it. But I was very lucky - on the first try the adjustment brought the watch to better than +1 sec/day. Accuracy has been rock stable since I made this change about 6 months ago. This highlights an advantage of getting an inexpensive watch -- I did not hesitate try this adjustment. Had I botched it (which is easy to do if not very careful .. if you poke past the adjustment lever into the hair spring, it will end up in a tangled, inoperable mess), I'd be out $50-60 bucks. So what! ... I could just get another! But I wouldn't consider, even for a moment, trying this with a watch I paid hundreds or thousands of $ for. So, ironically, precisely because I paid so little, I ended up watch that exceeds the Rolex 'superlative' chronometer standard (+-2 secs/day).

Note:There are various videos on Youtube that show how to regulate an automatic movement, including several specific to Seiko's 7S26.. This is not something that is for the faint of heart. It's not as easy as some people claim. It takes a steady hand and quite a bit of patience. -Don't try it with an expensive watch!

The movement has a power reserve of 43 hours. It's a good idea to test this shortly after purchase - once in a while there are problems with the rotor winding system, or mainspring/barrel assembly, resulting in limited power reserve -- these things are defects, cause for a return w/refund or repair under warranty. The "automatic" winding system should work well enough to get the watch completely would up after a day or two of full time wear. Swinging the watch gently and not too rapidly from side to side, face up, parallel to the ground, about 200 mm from side to side, can wind the watch up fully in a little over 5 minutes, I've found.

The 7S26 movement does not support manually winding with the crown, nor 'hacking', which means the second hand stopping when the crown is fully extended. These are convenient features that Seiko neglected to add to this entry level movement.
I've gotten used to it, as I have couple models with 7Sx6 movements. I'm willing to take a couple extra minutes to prepare these for wearing. Most people probably aren't as picky about making sure the watch is wound before setting the time, but I like to try to keep the watch as accurate as possible, and find just shaking it a few times isn't going to give the watch enough power to run optimally (till it's been worn for quite a while), so I 'wind them up' by swaying the watch side to side (face up, horizontal to the ground, about 200cm back and forth, gently, to imitate the arc the watch moves through when you walk), for as much as 3 or 4 minutes. Many people demonstrate getting an automatic movement going by shaking it like a rattle. This is probably not a good idea. It probably won't harm the watch unless you shake it pretty violently, but it's not the most efficient method. The watch is designed to wind fastest if you walk with it. So if you imitate the rate and range of a walking motion, it winds up fastest.

It's actually possible, also, to set the watch accurate to the second, even without hacking. If you rotate the crown a little bit backwards and stop, it will "pin" the second hand (stop it's movement). You have to continue putting a little pressure on the crown in the reverse direction till the real time catches up to the second hand. This doesn't work if the watch is almost fully wound. Sometimes the second hand starts going backwards - this is OK, and speeds up the the process of synchronized with time. It doesn't hurt the watch. Called "back hacking" it's a common practice.

Final thoughts:

Watch hobbyists have a saying, you hear it all the time: 'You can never go wrong with a Seiko'. This statement is meaningful. Seiko's products have consistent high quality, at all price points. The quality i in the materials used, in the finishing of the case, the quality of the crystal, the long lasting brightness of the substance applied to hands and markers to illuminate the the watch in the dark, and the distinctive approach taken to each model, making it unique and in it's own way, unlike any other. The high quality is hard to see in on-line photographs, and Seiko watches almost always seem a lot nicer when you "meet them in person". I think this is part of the 'you can't go wrong' stuff. If you like the on-line photos of a Sieko, but maybe aren't sure, you are not going to be disappointed with the actual watch. You will often adore it immediately, when you actually see it, and at the least not be disappointed.

top_rant.text:
Sometimes you try on a shoe that makes you wonder why you ever put anything else on your feet besides that shoe for all those years. THIS is one of those shoes. I got the Arcilla red bootie here: [[ASIN:B007Y52RKU Pikolinos Womens Le Mans Bootie Arcilla Size 38 EU(7.5-8 M US Women)]], and I also got the similarly styled Lead lace-up/zip up boot here: [[ASIN:B00Y224NI2 Pikolinos Women's Lead Le Mans 9232 38 M EU]]. I am going to review them together because they have a very similar fit and styling, plus I really think if you like one of these boots, you will like the other, too! And who doesn't love a good If/Then suggestion for great shoes? :-)

A little backstory on my feet, to give you reference of why I love these boots so much: I have always had wide feet with high arches. Over the years I moved away from buying the popular or cute shoes, unless they also fit my foot shape, as I was tired of hurting my toes in tight toe boxes or having my arches shoot angry hot spikes of pain if they were supported. However, the real drama began when I smashed my foot at work one day. The bruise covered the top of my foot, I thought it was broken and we did x-rays, but it was "just" my tendons. I may never know exactly what I did, but I literally could barely hobble from the bed to the bathroom for two weeks, then barely around the house for weeks more. I had to wear a pair of Dansko professional clogs *everywhere*, even around the house, as it was the only thing which supported my arch and delicate bones/tendons as everything down there healed.

Once my foot was mostly healed, I found I couldn't wear some of my shoes anymore. Even some Danskos were insufficient. I got rid of all my shoes that no longer would cut it, because the last thing I wanted was another two weeks bedridden and a month or more of hobbling around the house, let alone the worry of a lifetime of being potentially "crippled". So, since that time I've only bought shoes that have felt fantastic, and if they down the line failed to perform, support-wise, I ruthlessly culled them from my wardrobe. Some people have gotten some really nice shoes because of my wonky foot!

I've ended up with an odd mish-mash of favorites including some Ariats for horseback riding and barn tasks, some Fitflops for replacing all flip flops, some Danskos (slippers, clogs and a sneaker) and a couple pair of ABEO Ugg-a-likes for warm slippers. I have some orthotics and an array of light inserts, too. Although I am happy with the shoes I have, and not much of a shoe-hog/collector, etc., I needed some nicer shoes for work. It's casual work, so I didn't need fancy professional wear, but I wanted something casually classy and eventually after much searching I came across Pikolinos. I milled it over for awhile, considering the cost (not that much higher than my oft-worn Danskos) and if they would work out (buying from a new brand of shoes online is so risky!), but decided to bite the bullet since Amazon makes returns easy. I am SO SO SO glad I tried these! Here is why:

1. They are costly, but you can feel the quality. I do seriously wonder if all the ladies that got Chinese boots all bought knockoffs. My boots say "Made in Spain" and both look and feel more luxurious than any shoe I've ever worn. I've had plenty of shoes that looked nice and were well put-together, but these boots trump quality construction in that they are made from BUTTERY SOFT LEATHER! Literally, slide your foot in to the boots and you feel like you are sliding in to a leathery cloud. I've never felt shoes so plush, cushioned and leather so soft. Simultaneously, while this is not leather that you would want to ride a motorcycle in, the leather feels soft and expensive, but not thin by any means.

2. The design and style is fantastic. So the construction and components are all high-quality, but so is the look. From the textured, stacked heel to the perfect ergonomic toe box. From the piping and stitching on the heel, toe and instep to the adorable lace holders. The entire shoe (both of them - the booties and the zip up boot) both exude a vibe that says, "Someone lovingly designed my every detail".

3. Comfort! Comfort! Comfort! I know I already touched on this but this shoe is SO comfortable I can't get over it. I first slipped my (clean) bare foot into the boot to and it was so smooth inside. Like a polished leather sock. If this shoe fits you properly nothing should rub, even straight out of the box. Everything is ergonomic and supple leather that melts around your feet. There are no weird seams inside to rub or irritate the foot, just soft, smooth, flat, luxuriously supple leather.

4. The shoes may not be cheap, but considering the astounding quality, I think they are a bargain at this price and I am definitely a convert.

5. The insoles are removable. I haven't removed mine yet, because they have been so comfy, but I have considered removing them and testing them with my orthotics. If/when I do I will reply with an update.

Something of note: I don't know that I like the laces. They are stiff, thin and hard to tie so that it stays as they like to wiggle loose very easily. I also find them over-long. I may replace my laces, and if I do, I will reply to update this post with what I bought, and maybe an image or two! If you have suggestions I welcome a comment post! :-)

Tip: If your shoes don't make you feel amazing then you 1. Have the wrong size - or the shoe just isn't the right shape for your foot - sorry! :-( 2. You got a knock off. If they don't feel luxurious, if they say "Made in China", you may want to return them. I see notes of ladies saying "OMG! These shoes are great!" and "CHEAP! Made in CHINA!!! DON'T BUY!!". Well, don't buy the cheap ones that aren't Amazon Prime, try the shoes on at home, if they aren't great or you think they are fake - return them ASAP.

For reference: I have a somewhat wide foot. I usually prefer a C width, if available, but I can often wear a B width if it is wide cut enough, or soft. This shoe is both. It fits like a glove, in all the best ways, and I know it will conform perfectly over time. I usually wear a 7.5 US. If shoes are whole sizes I often go down to a 7, especially if the shoe is wide enough. In European sizes I typically wear a 38. I bought both the bootie and the zipper boot in 38 and they both fit great. There is a tiny bit of room at the toe, but this is where the toe tapers gently, and I prefer to not moosh my square foot into a tapered toe. If you have very narrow feet or toes that taper to a point you *may* be able to size down one size. Maybe.

Bottom line: I love love love love love LOVE these boots. I am so impressed with Pikolinos. I am definitely going to recommend them to others, both here and in person. I have to tell my mom about them and I am excited to try some of their other shoes in the future. Highly recommended!

Again, I got the bootie and the zip up boot. Both fit and feel almost exactly the same. Both are highly recommended. If you like one I bet you will like the other. The red is gorgeous, but the black (lead) is a great black with a nice character to it in that it is very dark grey, mottled a bit with the brown shoe lace stays. SO beautiful!

P.S. I am going to add a few pictures. I am adding the appropriate pictures to each shoe. If you like THIS shoe, please check out my other review for the pictures of that shoe, too. :-) Best of luck and hope this helped!

UPDATE 05/06/2016: I am in love with this boot. It has to be one of my favorite shoes ever. I slip it on and it feels like a buttery cloud of leather that super gently hugs my foot in the best possible way - as though someone handcrafted this shoe to my foot, bespoke-style. It's comfortable, it's so cool looking, it goes with EVERYTHING! I've been wearing them almost daily since I got them and they look lovely despite rain-storms, mud and dogs - though even encountering these things could have harmed them I was appropriately cautious as opposed to just stomping around and scuffing into things. Highly recommended you give these babies a shot if you have any interest in them, and have similar B to edging towards C width feet. <3 <3 <3 them!
top_rant.title: Most buttery, soft, exquisite-quality boot I've worn in my life! 5+ Stars!

top_rant.text:
I just LOVE these divers from Invicta! I have a number of them, but, until now, did not have one in this design, not only in the all black, but with the "open heart" movement, so, in looking to get a simple black and silver automatic diver, I was very pleased to come across this one. This kind of finishes out my Invicta diver collection (although I do have one more on my list), and it's beautiful piece. Although listed as a "Pro Diver" this model is actually a "Grand Diver," it should be noted.

For those considering the brand, please don't be swayed by erroneous complaints and negativity. It is a solid brand, and especially in model lines such as this diver series, you're going to get a great watch. I began, in watch collecting, with Invicta, and I don't collect to sell or trade, I collect watches because I love them and, after buying just a few, many long years ago, it became a bit of a hobby and passion to learn what makes a good watch and what makes one worth buying. I'll always be thankful to this brand for launching me into a real love of watches.

Obviously, this is not a "brag" brand, but many of us are not interested in trying to impress a meaningless crowd, nor do we have tens of thousands to blow on wrist wear. Thankfully, one doesn't need to spend a fortune to get terrific watchery, and, although I eventually expanded out from Invicta to explore other watch makers, and now own a host of "true Swiss" and even "Swiss luxury" watches, along with nice pieces from the Far East, I haven't found it necessary to spend more than a few thousand, ever, on a watch to get a really brilliant, top quality piece, and, in fact, I'd say the majority of watches I own have been procured for under a grand. Even so, despite exploring many other makers, I still find Invicta to have worthy offerings to add.

In terms of quality, while people generalize a faulty watch received as a blanket statement of "bad quality control," I will say that any review of any brand, including "high end" supposedly perfect watches, can turn up dissatisfied customers, so it happens across the board, and doesn't make it a crap shoot to buy one of these. I've bought a lot of Invictas over the years, and haven't received any in anything but excellent condition. I know other watch enthusiasts who have bought far more than I have, and, again, zero bad watches received. My brother has bought quite a few, and, once more, no bad experiences. I'm not dismissing, at all, the fact that some people have had less than happy times with getting a watch that has issues, but I'm just saying that such doesn't fault the entire brand as a total failure, any more than any other brand.

Customer service issues? Yes, and, again, other brands have issues as well, so no one is perfect. I've only ever even needed service, twice, so that's saying something already about quality, in that a service call is a rare call to have to make. The calls I needed? Once from an accidental drop of a watch over a cliff face, resulting in a loose index marker (fixed and returned in less than two weeks) and the other was a quartz movement that died (the watch was about ten years old) which I would say was a failure in terms of longevity, to some degree, given the expected lifespan of a quartz Ronda movement. That replacement took seven months, because it had to be sourced from Switzerland, and was not a movement still in wide circulation, so it not easy to locate one. But, in both cases, Invicta came through.

So, it's not always a case of spending more getting you more and, in any case, in terms of price, it's not just a matter of having the money to spend, or not, but more so I just don't see the justification of mega price tags on many watches -- after all, sapphire is still sapphire, water resistance is still water resistance and a good movement is still a good movement. None of that becomes augmented by paying twenty grand as opposed to a thousand bucks, or even far less and, beyond watches that cost a million dollars, many ten grand plus watches aren't even really that interesting.

Thankfully, however, many watches houses are dedicated to affordable excellence and, while hyper-expensive watches do serve in their capacity to be rare and unique in some cases, for the most part you can have a phenomenal watch ownership experience for a very reasonable price tag. Brands like Certina, Victorinox, Louis Erard, Christopher Ward, Hamilton, Raymond Weil, and many more have all served me well in stunning pieces that aren't the cost of a small car.

Where does Invicta fit into this picture? Well, one should always know what they are looking for, and know what they are getting. Within the brand, there is a wide range, from budget pieces to COSC certified ones and even to models sporting the legendary Valjoux 7750 engine (which is used by some of the world's most expensive brands). Generally, you will pay far, far less with Invicta, to get a solid time piece than many other brands and, while there are cases of "getting what you pay for," in terms of the quality not being top drawer, my experience has very often been the reverse, of an Invicta delivering a watch that is much, MUCH more than what you've paid, particularly versus competing brands.

One criticism of Invicta is the MSRP tags that show on the watch, being cited as overly inflated, to try to manipulate a buyer into thinking they got a great deal on the actual purchase price. To that, I'd say, first of all, who cares about any inflated price, when what matters is what you actually are required to pay. It's far worse to have a price of ten grand, for a watch that isn't ten grand worth of watch, and actually pay that price, versus buying a watch for a few hundred dollars that is actually worth more than every penny, no matter how much it is said to have been discounted. I mean, if one feels better to spend more, and get less, that's fine, but I'd rather spend less, and get more.

The other side of that coin is the reality of the MSRP, which is actually not offered as a manipulation, but rather as a point of comparison, for consideration of value for money. What Invicta is suggesting is simply how much you might be charged by other brands, for the same thing you're getting. So, for example, the MSRP tag that came with this model is $695. An open heart design automatic watch can easily run you that much, and more. Now, you're not getting a sapphire crystal here, but consider the Tissot open heart automatic, which also comes with a mineral crystal, same as this one, but has minimal water resistance and still costs nearly six hundred dollars.

So, the point is, Invicta is advising that you certainly could spend more, to get this kind of watch, but they aren't asking more. Far from being a slam against the brand, I think it just proves Invicta's pledge of bringing quality watch ownership without the crazy prices.

In the brand line, these divers are almost the pinnacle of this pledge, with most of them being available for around a hundred dollars. They even do have editions with sapphire (check out their Sea Base line -- amazing!) and, in most cases, they have a terrific Seiko movement inside, like this one, which is reliable and accurate and sturdy, plus the watches are made with solid materials, they fit great and look incredible.

Because I'm familiar with these, this one was just another consistent example of the diver series. Given that larger styles are in keeping with today's watches, they size is perfect here -- wonderful wrist presence but still fits nicely under a cuffed shirt and suit jacket. Simple but elegant styling means you can dress it up, or dress it down, so it's very versatile. Comfort on these divers is superb, particularly with the slight curve on the case lugs. The combination of brushed and polished surfaces is very attractive, and the textured black dial with sweeping second hand, with the Invicta logo counterbalance, and, of course, the exposed movement, make for a hypnotizing view. Indices are highlighted nicely in polished silver, the hands are very easy to read and the 120 click bezel performs flawlessly as an added time-keeping measure.

No date window, but I knew that in purchasing it, and I like the unique face here. It's nice to wear a watch with a distinct look. Lume is not super great, but no less than most watches that are not "lume" oriented. What I do love about the lume is that it is BLUE! Looks awesome when the lights go out, and very cool that they went with blue instead of the usual green glow. Water resistance is very respectable at a thousand feet and the exhibition case base is a very beautiful touch. Bracelet is strong, with a reliable fold over clasp.

The only drawback here? Well, it seems a bit of a reversal of my review, perhaps, but I'd say I wish I'd picked it up for less, and I only say that because, ordinarily, I wouldn't pay a few hundred dollars for one of these divers simply because they usually run closer to a hundred, or maybe a hundred and thirty dollars. But, I didn't come across this until just recently, and Invicta is no longer producing it apparently, so I was a little bit too late coming to the party on this one, meaning costs are higher now, to acquire one. I've came across some listings around 140 or so, but those have all been sold out for some time.

So, at three hundred dollars, it's less of a steal, but that's a minor point and not a complaint, since it's my own fault for not getting this when prices were lower. It's just a testament to how much it's still a deal, even at the current price, that I can still say it's more than worth it. As with many of these, they do sell out and are gone, forever, so I felt lucky that there were still some of these around, at all, since I really fell in love with it at first sight, and, in person, it's even more amazing.

In the end, five stars, all day long, and well deserved, in my opinion -- quality, value, beautiful fit and finish, and just a terrific time piece. As a gift to yourself or someone else, you'll be thoroughly pleased.

I highly recommend!

Thanks for reading, and I hope this has been helpful.

top_rant.text:
I'm a bit surprised by the number of reviews that say the quality of this luggage is poor. I've had two of this style of bag now - granted they were earlier models than this. Hopefully the reviews weren't specific about this exact luggage (mine looked a bit different aesthetically but it's the same bag). But a long review commences here as I have to stick up for this bag. Maybe some other people received "lemons". I can't attest to High Sierra warranty topics but I don't know if it applies with Amazon's varying services.

I travel a lot for work. How much? Over the last number of years my territory has been between 14 and 28 states. It's not uncommon for me to have 6 flights over 3 days. So the luggage sees a lot of airline miles and as everyone knows those are rough miles (don't get me started on how bad airlines workers are with managing our bags). Typically my bag is maxed out on top of it. I have a reputation of showing up for work with everything including the kitchen sink....plus a spare. This bag holds it all, which is why I really like them. Only about 2 times have I ever needed to bring a second bag on a trip. My team is always amazed at what I show up with to work out of state. They really don't ask me anymore "where did you get that from" because they've all seen my bag now....which they have deemed affectionately "the Traveling Tent". They like to tell people I live in it and it's almost big enough. How much does it hold? Well, whatever volume the bag is I've had it filled - to where I had to sit on it to get it to zip up. Weight-wise I can tell you this thing has been tagged by the airlines with the extra "warning - heavy" tags placed on it. The heaviest load I can remember came in about 85 pounds on the airport scale.

So, with all that - I've always been amazed it hasn't blown up from how much junk I put in them and also factoring in the airline idiots really get their jollies off of beating up everyone's luggage. My first bag like this lasted...maybe 3-4 years? I had it so long I can't remember. I only know the ONLY reason it was retired was an unfortunate event regarding a very large Red Bull, bottle of multivitamins and large bag of candy left over from work I through in the bag last minute without thinking as I was close to missing a flight. Result...(after airlines kicking the crap out of the bag)....was a smell in the bag that didn't wash out so I had to put it to rest. At that point, it was only the smell that made me replace it. I saw one reviewer write about the broken plastic clip that fastens the top part of the bag and the lower. One of mine had broken too (over 3-4 years). IMO it's only aesthetic, so I removed the other part and cut off the straps. Problem solved - functionality all the same. All the zippers were still in working order. The extendable handle to pull the bag with had also finally gone out (I blame shuttle bus/cab/Uber folks for this). But I didn't care. The strap situated by where the handle pulled out worked equally as well to tote it around. I've never used the back pack straps - so I can't accurately say I know how they work, etc., IMO this was just an extra compartment I could stuff skinny stuff in.

So I bought another one. I was excited as these bags aren't super common already (you'll never get them mistaken with another person's luggage) and the "new" version I was buying came in this bright red color so it was easier for me to spot when retrieving luggage. For the record - it was THIS red bag that I broke my weight record with (mentioned above). I've had this bag for I guess a year or so now? And I'm looking to replace it. Why? If you haven't figured out I'm not super happy with how the airlines treat our property (seen enough bags being loaded by the airline workers at this point). Somehow the airlines managed to drop/ding/run my luggage into something, I don't know what, but there were scrape marks on my bag in the damaged area...and they had jacked up so bad that it actually ripped the stitching from the lower zipper out in a section about 3 inches long (picture of red bag). The zipper itself didn't break at all! It was scratched...and when closing the zipper through that section it would hang up a bit...but zipper worked fine. Somehow the airline had done something to actually break out the stitching of the zipper. This wasn't something that can be sewed. The actual zip section tore away.

Some people pamper luggage. I don't. I'm 6-3+. 230 pounds. Even when this luggage shows 60+ pounds at the airport I "rag doll" this bag around. How the sewed on top handle or straps haven't broken yet I don't know...or why this bag hasn't totally blown up. I whip it from the sewed on top handle into the back of my truck. I yank the handles and anything else I can grab to get it into car trunks. I whip the bag with the top handle to wherever in the hotel I deem is it's resting place during my stay. I don't know if it's water resistant but I've even towed it through Louisiana downpours where I ended up soaked but somehow MOST of my clothes were perfectly dry. It's a tough bag and I have to thank High Sierra for their "Terry" resistant manufacturing here. Most things aren't "Terry" proof.

So, there it is. I'll stand by this bag and recommend to everyone. That's why I'm getting another one (hoping quality didn't go down like I said above). Why 4 stars then? Because I can't give 4.5. What are my negative points?
- This may not bother some, but I'm a bit O.C. See that picture with the carrying straps? It's a velcro "handle" at the top. This has happened for each bag. The velcro wears out like yesterday?! So when I leave my house I neatly strap the two straps together with the velcro handle. I'll be dang'ed if by the time I wheel it through the house to the trunk of my car and put it in....the two side straps are flapping around. When I get my luggage at the airport...the straps are flapping. When Uber, taxi, shuttle, or my rental car gets my luggage...the straps are flapping when I take it out. Ugh. Fix that somehow! Maybe use some of that Tungsten thread/stitching they use on the rest of the bag to at least sew in a couple extra "tie downs" so I can tie the handles together if needed...
- Color. I said above I really liked the bright red color of my bag. I do. But, after about 1 year of use the dirt/airline abuse/everything else luggage goes through is showing more than my bag that was 3-4 years old. The red color is too light and it shows all the dirt and junk. My first generation bag was straight black and looked better after 4 years (didn't show all the jacked up stuff) than my newer bag that didn't get as much use. If I had an option (apparently not now) I'd go back to a dark, solid black color knowing after 5 years or more I've only seen one other bag like this spinning around on the luggage carousel. The color options now are going to show airline "affection".
- On my newer bag...one of those high performance thick cables through the zippers came off. My last bag I had for ~4 years still had those. Maybe I'm being too picky on that.
- Loose the back pack straps perhaps. Any sane person wouldn't use this for a back pack. People can buy a "real" back pack with this much volume if they need it. I just don't see most folks using it. Just keep the zipper part as it's an extra pouch. Maybe that drops the cost a bit?
- Yeah, I'm dinging for 1/2 a point on that stuff or 1 point on the Amazon scale. You decide if that's too harsh or not.

Other random topics - someone asked about using for backpacking through Europe. Ok...?! Maybe you should get an actual back pack? This isn't contoured and overall I would say not designed for using as a long term back pack. I've had my 35 pound computer/carry on bag, 50 pound tool bag and this bag to travel with. Not once did i ever have to put this on as a backpack - they make back packs with this much volume so go buy one of those if you want a back pack. Don't ding this product because you are a granola-head looking for a cheaper option for something you actually intend to use as a back pack - common sense stuff here. Hard luggage is better? No. One of my team sent a picture of their hard luggage that had a 6 inch bomb-hole in the side. And it was expensive luggage. And find hard luggage this big...and compare the price. I've "exported" fine food from Louisiana back home with me in this soft luggage (I always keep some 1 gallon ziplock bags in the luggage) and thoughtfully/carefully packed it in the lower section and never had any of it get busted open - minus the incident mentioned above. That's a miracle frankly but I'll take it.

So in summary - not to drag this on I just have to challenge a lot of the 1 star ratings - you'll be hard pressed to use one of these bags as often as I do...and even harder pressed to put as much weight and volume into them as I do. And this product has been great for me. Price - are you kidding? I don't think there's anything at this price (still not cheap) that can hold this much. After posting this, I'm clicking on buttons to purchase a new one from Amazon. If for some reason these newer versions don't make the grade I'll definitely update this review. If you don't see anything...it's performing as good as I would expect it would.

top_rant.text:
I am redoing my review having now had this bag for well over a year. Still going with five stars but I'll add more information and observations after 18 months of use.

I use the pack for just about everything. In fact I just got a second one in Rye colors so that I don't have to swap things around but still don't get the bags mixed up. (Showing up at school with your gym stuff or vice versa isn't a good idea IMHO.)

First, durability. I beat the heck out of my stuff. I beat stuff so badly in fact that my wife complains about the number I do on my pants, shirts... well everything. I carry this bag with me everywhere. School, the gym, BJJ etc. Until recently I only had the one bag so that's really what I'm basing this review on. For durability I give it 5/5. It's held up way, way better than any of the Kelty bags I had as an undergrad. At first I was worried about the metal clips pulling out of the material but that worry has proved to be unfounded. Zippers are high quality as well. Really, after ~18 months the bag shows no signs of wear at all. The seams show exactly 0 signs of wear or pulling out. I will say this however: Check your zippers when you first get the bag. Oakley doesn't make them, they just stitch them into the bag. The first Rye colored bag (my second Mechanism pack) I got had to be sent back because the zipper on one of the top pockets was improperly manufactured (Upon inspection this was a defect in the slider). Oakley didn't catch it. Don't worry though, Amazon will have another bag on the way to you in 24 hours and all you have to do is print a label and send the original bag back within 30 days. After it was replaced I had no problems with that bag either. Having worked in manufacturing I can tell you that while annoying, this sort of thing happens to every company, even the very, very top end companies.

Second, features: It's a rucksack style bag so you have to understand that (more on that later). The laptop slot seems like it won't fit a 17" laptop but it will once the bag is broken in. (When I first got it the bag was a bit stiff, but it's broken in very nicely and become extremely comfortable. The stiffness also made it seem like a 17" laptop wouldn't fit. It works itself out.) The main compartment, properly packed will carry five days worth of clothing without a problem as long as you only carry three pairs of heavy pants (think flannel lined jeans with regular jeans or other pants you can fit in a pair for each day). With shorts, five pairs no problem, you'll have extra room. If you're not used to a ruck style pack you might find this compartment difficult to organize with school stuff. If you have that problem I recommend something like a Maxpedition pocket organizer for your pens/pencils/stapler/other small stuff. The main compartment will hold that organizer, three largish textbooks two Five Star 5 subject notebooks and a laptop without trouble. Power cords, phones, Ipods, a rain shell, and a ton of other stuff will still fit in the outside pockets.

The bag also has seven outside pockets four on the sides and two on top. The top two are lined to not scratch electronics and one of them has a port for your headphones should you choose to put your iPod/MP3 player/phone in it. The top pockets are medium sized. They will not fit a full sized smartphone with case. The top side pockets are also medium but larger than the top, top pockets. Each one will accommodate something like a 4" Israeli Bandage plus a large compass. The lower side pockets will take a 1L water bottle or a rolled up raincoat without issue. (Picture 2 w/ 1.25L Diet Coke Bottle) The main front pocket is actually huge. It will hold A LOT. For example if you wear board short style swim trunks you could roll them up and put 4-6 pairs in that pocket. That pocket on my pack contains a First Aid kit that measures 7 and 3/8 inches high 5 and 1/4 inches wide by 3" deep plus a package of Wet Ones, a whole roll of medical wrap, a 6" Israeli Bandage (in complete packaging) and a roll of medical tape (Far right foreground picture 3). The pocket is about 90% full. I also keep a rainshell in a lower outside pocket, a 1.5L water bottle in the other, a 4" Israeli bandage in an upper side pocket along with a compass, power cables and a flashlight in the other upper side pocket and I still have the "shelf pocket" and two top pockets for whatever else I want. Yeah, this bag holds a TON of stuff.

As for the metal clips... OK, I know some people rag on this as "poser technical" even in the reviews and questions here on Amazon. They know not of what they speak. These clips are far superior to plastic if you live somewhere it actually gets cold in the winter. I've had pastic clips from just about every brand other than Arc'Teryx break in cold weather or get iced up and be unusable without melting the ice. Spending Arc'Teryx type money on a daily pack is stupid beyond belief. Don't get me wrong, I LOVE my Muira 50 from Arc'Teryx but that's a $400+ technical climbing bag, not appropriate for daily carry and not something you want to have grow legs. [If you live somewhere where Arc'Teryx stuff is common thieves know what it is and will target you for a bag like that.] These clips are easy to use and they last far better than plastic. Ditto the other metal reinforcements on the bag. Don't listen to the haters. Those metal doodads are there for a reason and they serve that purpose quite well.

Third, ease of use. This bag is incredibly easy to organize once you get used to a using a ruck style pack (if you're not already). Yes, for clothing and whatnot you have to think about it a bit in terms of not putting stuff you need often at the bottom of the main compartment but that's true for all rucks.

Forth, weather resistance. This bag is water resistant out of the box. However, if you're carrying a laptop or cameras you might want to upgrade that water resistance. I put two coats of Scotch Guard on this thing and it's basically waterproof. I tried soaking it down with a hose in my yard and unless I opened the top flap and sprayed water directly into the main compartment that laptop was going to be safe (I filled it with crumpled paper for this test and checked to see if any of the paper got wet.) Make sure you Scotch Guard the bottom of the bag too in case you drop her in a puddle or something. (Scotch Guard is cheap so go heavy with it if you like.)

Bottom Line:

This is a fantastic backpack for all sorts of uses. EDC, camping, get home bag, gym bag, school bag, range bag or just carting stuff around. It's comfortable, well made and easy to organize. A little Scotch Guard and it's as waterproof as you're gonna get before you go up $100 or more. It's fairly low profile in a city environment so if a "get home bag" is what you're looking for it's a good one. It's not covered in MOLLE/PALS and unless you get the camo one it doesn't scream "I OPERATE SO HARD!!!" when you're wandering around with it. This bag is good enough that after 18 months of beating the crap out of it I bought a second one in a different color just to have two of them. It's a FANTASTIC bag. If you want to hang stuff on the outside of your pack look at something like the Kitchen Sink.

The bag is also tough as nails. You can abuse it. You can pack it to the gills without worrying about busting a seam, zipper or pulling out a metal doodad anchor. For the prices you reguarly find on amazon, $100-$140 you really can't go wrong with this bag if it covers what you want/need it to cover.

What it's not:

It's not a hardcore technical mountaineering pack. It wasn't designed to be and it's not useful for such a purpose. If you're looking at it for that you're barking up the wrong tree. Of course if you're looking at it for such a purpose you need your head examined in the first place because Oakley doesn't make stuff like that.

It will carry all the stuff you see pictured (Picture 1) plus a bunch more and that's BEFORE you hit the main compartment. It will carry what you see in picture 3 EASILY for travel. That's a king size bed covered in stuff and it all packs into this bag with ease.

Also included because the seller doesn't include one, is a picture of the main compartment. (Picture 4)

top_rant.text:
[[VIDEOID:c0a4088794c0140ba4fe82989d4ddeae]] For starters, you should know that while I bought these boots to wear specifically for hiking the Inca trail to Machu Picchu in Peru, I am actually a professional hiker for a living and have tried ALL. THE. BOOTS. and ALL. THE. HIKING SHOES. I have a part time job taking peoples dogs off leash pack hiking in the mountains around the Salt Lake Valley where I live. I've been doing this for about 4 years now every day through every season (rain, snow and blazing desert summer sun) and have been through a lot of boots and shoes, so I know what's good and what isn't. I have also completed hikes such as mount Timpanogos and Half Dome among others.

My very first pair of hiking boots when I started this job were the Hoka One Speedgoat Mid trail runners and I loved them. . . while they lasted. Unfortunately, one of the drawbacks to Hoka boots and shoes that anyone with real experience will tell you is that those amazing thick and cushy luxury EVA soles they have just don't hold up for a long time. That's really unfortunate considering how expensive they are, which is why I never bought another pair after my Speed Goats degraded after one season.

I switched to Salomon boots and hiking shoes which are my favorite every day hikers and have been for years now. They are well made, comfortable and hold up to lots of abuse. That being said, my Salomon winter boots are not only getting old, but they're pretty rigid, and not as comfortable for my feet as I wanted for the Inca trail. I turned 40 the day we summited Machu Picchu and foot fatigue is my biggest battle on trail, so I wanted every advantage I could get in the shoe department.

I can't tell you how many shoes and boots I tried on before settling on these for my trip, but they included Keen, Salomon Predict (which were my second place finishers), Altra Lonepeak (DON'T DO IT) and Merrill among others. Here were my criteria:

- The softest, most comfortable boots I could possibly find
- COMPLETELY waterproof in reality, not just on paper
- Durable
- Super traction
- Laces that stay laced and don't drive me bonkers
- Don't look totally ugly
- Require very little break in time

These boots checked every single box for me and then some, and trust me, I put them TO THE TEST! Not only did I break them in hiking through the snow and rocks of Utah before my trip, but after the Inca trail, I wore them in the Peruvian Amazon jungle and to the desert oasis of Huacachina. They were absolute BEASTS!! Other people in my group were complaining about foot pain, foot fatigue, blisters, hot spots, wet feet, you name it! But me and my friend who bought the big sister of the Anacapa, the Sky Kaha, had ZERO complaints!

Some highlights that set these boots apart from the rest:

- 100% waterproof. Just TRUST!

- GREAT grip and traction

- You will not find boots with a more comfortable sole that shields your feet from feeling all the rocks and tree roots and trail debris you step on. This is why I HATE Lonepeak boots and shoes (and I've tried them)! They are super comfortable until you're actually out on trail in the real world. The soles just aren't thick and rigid enough to protect your feet from what you're stepping on. You won't necessarily notice it in the moment, but trust me, that adds up to lots of foot fatigue when the miles pile on!

- FABULOUS laces and lacing system! I really love the locking laces the enable you to customize where the boots are tight and where they are looser. This is a game changer for people like me who tend to get numb toes when boots are laced too tightly over the bridge of my foot.

- Super well made and durable

- Super supportive! I've had several ankle rolls while hiking in these boots and none of them actually hurt or weren't easily walked off. In other boots or shoes they surely would've cause more damage or possible injury.

- LIGHT WEIGHT! I defy you to find a waterproof boot this supportive that weighs as little as these.

- Unique and cool looking! I love the color!

- Fit is great, and I was able to buy my true size! I normally wear 8.5 in shoes and ended up with an 8.5 in these as well. Normally I buy boots half a size larger to account for sock layering and leaving room for my toes to not hit the front of the boots during steep descents, but Hoka seems to have accounted for that for me and my toes never touched the front.

- Little to no break in time. I wouldn't say these really took any breaking in, just more getting used to.

- Easy to clean. These clean up really nicely with a wet cloth and look great again!

The thing you should anticipate before you try or buy these is that they will feel different than any other boots you try on. They are extremely chunky, the soles will make you taller and the way the sole sticks out from the heel might feel odd at first and take some getting used to. I think their bulk and unique soles put some people off at first because it feels a little weird until you get used to it, but I'm telling you right now, THAT'S THE BEST PART!! Don't be off put by the unusual feeling until you give them a fair shot. Your feet will thank you! Also, that extra chunk makes them a lot more of a pain in the butt to pack, but again, WORTH IT.

So, what about the degradation of the EVA soles that I mentioned in the beginning? Well, I'm fully anticipating that will happen again with these. That being said, they are looking almost as good as new after Peru and 2 months of local hiking, so I will have to update when they do start to degrade. BUT even if and when they do, I must say that at least for a big hike like the Inca trail, they were worth EVERY PENNY. In fact, even if they only last me a couple years on my local trails, I'm at an age now where the protection from the impact of hiking on my feet, knees, ankles and hips that these boots offer is worth the price, as steep as that is, even if they don't last as long as other brands and models. I've learned that with hiking boots and shoes, everything is a tradeoff, and for me, comfort is king. So, if I can buy the most comfortable boots out there but they don't last as long as less comfortable boots, I'll take the comfortable ones every day of the week and twice on Sundays.

Speaking of price, I bought mine locally for $185 (which wasn't a sale - that was full price) for reference.

Since I've returned from Peru, I have been hiking my favorite shoes again (Salomon XA Pro 3D) that I hiked Half Dome in, just because it's summer and hotter and I like lighter, more minimal shoes. But I have to say, after spending a few months in the Anacapa's my feet got SPOILED and even in my favorite hiking shoes of all time I noticed a significant amount of additional foot fatigue that I have not had since wearing my Anacapa's, and that's just in short 5 mile hikes! HOKAs will spoil your feet in the best possible way haha. Now I think I have to buy some HOKA hiking shoes for summers!

One more pro tip for the ULTIMATE foot comfort of serious hikers, ultra or trail runners and backpackers. After lots and lots of testing and experimentation with different combinations, I feel like I have stumbled upon the perfect "recipe" for happy feet on trail, free from fatigue, blisters, calluses, hot spots, sweaty feet, chafing, ALL OF IT. Here it is, you may thank me profusely later.

Layer 1 - Injiinji crew sock liners WITH INDIVIDUAL TOES. Not the socks, the sock liners. The individually wrapped toes make all the difference.
Layer 2 - Dr. Fredericks gel toe caps (for use on big toes OVER the sock liners)
Layer 3 - A pair of Darn Tough FULL CUSHION boot socks
Layer 4 - Hoka One Anacapa (or Sky Kaha) boots

I sincerely hope this review has been helpful to someone else! And yes, I paid FULL PRICE for these. Feel free to ask any questions. Happy trails, friends!

JANUARY 2014 UPDATE:

BELOW 20 DEGREES WITH WIND AND I CLEAR 10 DRIVEWAYS WEARING UNDER-ARMOR KNOCK-OFFS, SHORTS, FLANNEL SHIRT, AND CARHARTT EXTREME COVERALLS. AT 5 DEGREES I WORE FLANNEL SHIRT, DICKIE SLACKS, AND THE EXTREME COVERALLS.....AND WAS TOO HOT! BETTER WAY TO SAY IT; I HAVEN'T BEEN CHILLED OR COLD WEARING THESE.

I DIDN'T REALLY NEED THESE WITH THE OTHER CARHARTT GEAR I HAVE (and can't wear out) BUT MY WIFE SAYS I CAN SURRENDER TO FASHION ONCE IN A WHILE. ALSO, IT WAS ONE OF THE FEW TIMES A CARHARTT PRODUCT WAS HEAVILY DISCOUNTED (50%-UNHEARD OF). THERE IS A REASON FOR THAT, ACCORDING TO ONE OF MY LOCAL WORK WEAR RETAILERS. ACCORDING TO HIM, "BIG AND BULKY IS OUT. UNDER ARMOR (AND THEIR KNOCK-OFF COUSINS) HAS REVOLUTIONIZED THE INDUSTRY AND THE CONSUMER WANTS THIN LAYERS NOT BIG/BULKY."

HE HAS A GOOD POINT (and he has been at it a long time) AND I OWN A NUMBER OF "UNDER ARMOR" KNOCK-OFF ITEMS. FACE IT, THE ONLY DIFFERENCE BETWEEN ITEMS OF 95% POLYESTER/5% SPANDEX IS THE BRAND NAME, PACKAGING, AND MARKETING BUDGET. "UNDER ARMOR" COSTS MORE THAN "NIKE PRO-COMBAT", WHICH COSTS MORE THAN "CABELLA'S POLAR-TECH", WHICH COSTS MORE THAN "REBOCK", WHICH..... I THINK YOU'RE CATCHING ON. (hell, go to wal-mart and get "starter" stuff and tape an "under armor" logo to it)

BUT.....WHEN I WAS A DECKHAND ON A TOWBOAT (before the turn of the century) I LIKED TO BE ABLE TO SLIP ON A PAIR OF LOOSE FITTING COVERALLS OVER MY WORK CLOTHES AND BE COMFORTABLE CHECKING BARGES, MAKING LOCKS, ETC.. THIS ITEM REMINDS ME OF AN OLD SCHOOL SNOW MOBILE SUIT. IT IS MADE FOR COLD, REPEAT, COLD WEATHER WHEN IT IS DOUBTFUL THERE IS MEANINGFUL WORK TO BE DONE, ANYWAY. YOU WON'T DIG A DITCH IN THESE BUT YOU WILL BE COMFORTABLE INSPECTING A WATER MAIN BREAK IN JANUARY. WHEN I ORDERED THEM IN LATE FEBRUARY, I DIDN'T THINK I WOULD WEAR THEM THIS WINTER, HOWEVER, THEY CAME IN VERY HANDY ON A BREEZY, 20-25 DEGREE MARCH DAY WHEN I HAD TO DO AN OUTDOOR INSPECTION. THE INVICTA IS A GOOD WATCH FOR THE MONEY. I HAVE 8,OF VARIOUS STYLES AND MOVEMENTS. I ALSO HAVE ROLEX, ELGIN, SEIKO(AUTOMATICS). THE INVICTA IS ABOUT 15-20 SECONDS/ DAY ACCURATE. THE ROLEX AND SEIKOS ABOUT 5-7 SECONDS / ACCURATE, THE ELGINS 16-25 SECONDS/DAY ACCURATE. THE ONLY REAL PROBLEM I HAVE HAD WITH THE INVICTAS IS THE CROWN. MAYBE THE METAL IS SOFTER THAN MY OTHER WATCHES, I DO NOT KNOW. THE PROBLEM IS THAT THE THREADS ,AFTER ABOUT 10 MONTHS TO ONE YEAR WILL NOT SEAT PROPERLY OR THREAD TIGHTLY, THEREFORE RENDERING THE WATCH USELESS AS A WATERPROOF MODEL. I AM VERY CAREFUL AND DILIGENT ABOUT RE-TIGHTENING THE CROWN AFTER RESETTING,TO AVOID CROSS-THREADING THE CROWN AND WATCH BASE,YET IT HAS HAPPENED WITH 4 OF THE WATCHES. MY MOST USED WATCH, A SEIKO AUTOMATIC DAY/DATE HAS NO SUCH PROBLEM AND IS NEARLY AS ACCURATE AS MY ROLEX THUNDERBIRD AND ROLEX SUBMARINER. I HAVE HAD NO PROBLEMS WITH MY ROLEX OR SEIKO WATCHES , WITH REGARDS TO TIMEKEEPING OR CROWN STRIPPING. I LIKE THE ROLEX SUBMARINER,S LARGER CROWN MAKING IT EASIER TO RE-THREAD. HOWEVER, THE ROLEX THUNDERBIRD AND THE SEIKO DAY/DATE HAVE SMALL THREADED CROWNS AND HAVE HAD NO STRIPPING PROBLEMS. INVICTA, MAY WANT TO CHECK THEIR THREADING SIZE OR CROWN MATERIAL. THE WATCHES THEMSELVES ARE GREAT -DAY- TO -DAY WATCHES FOR ABUSE,GARDENING, SPORTS, OR PRETENSE,( THAT THEY ARE A REAL ROLEX). MANY PEOPLE I KNOW HAVE THOUGHT THAT THEY WERE ROLEX. THE INVICTA BLUE STANDS OUT MUCH MORE THAN THE ROLEX,AND THE GOLD PLATING SURPRISING,LASTS QUITE A LONG TIME. YOU CANNOT GO WRONG WITH THIS WATCH FOR THE MONEY,IF YOU ONLY EXPECT IT TO LAST A YEAR OR TWO,AND DO NOT MIND RESETTING IT EVERY WEEK(AUTOMATICS) OR EVERY OTHER MONTH,(QUARTZ). IF YOU LOSE IT OVERBOARD,SOMEHOW BREAK IT OR ARE ROBBED , YOU ARE NOT OUT THOUSANDS OF DOLLARS. THE SEIKOS ARE MORE EXPENSIVE,SOMETIMES BY A FEW HUNDRED DOLLARS,DO NOT RESEMBLE A ROLEX,BUT KEEP BETTER TIME,LAST LONGER. SOME FROM THE EARLY 1970S COULD TAKE 5-6 G-FORCES, TROPICAL HEAT, AND GET DUNKED IN "YANKEE STATION" AND KEPT TICKING ACCURATELY ALONG. THEY WERE TRADED IN ALONG WITH AN OMEGA SEAMASTER FOR THE ROLEX SUBMARINER.
FOR THE MONEY-INVICTAS ARE "BRAVO ZULU" ( "WELL - DONE" )FOR DAILY PEACE-TIME USE. FOR NEAR COMPLETE RELIABLITY IN HOSTILE SITUATIONS GO FOR THE ROLEX, OMEGA, BREITLING, OR THE BEST SEIKO AUTOMATIC WITH "HACK" FEATURE THAT YOU CAN AFFORD, YOUR LIFE MAY DEPEND UPON ITS ACCURACY, AND RUGGEDNESS.I AM PURCHASING ANOTHER FAUX ROLEX-INVICTA AND DEEP-SIXING THE ONE WITH A "CROWN DEPARTURE." "HANDSOME" HANSON

THE SHOES WERE RECOMMENDED TO ME BY MY FOOT SURGEON WHEN I WENT TO HIM WITH EXCRUCIATING PAIN IN MY FEET AND ANKLES. HE SAID I NEEDED ARCH SUPPORT EVERY TIME I GOT OUT OF BED AND IF I DIDN'T WANT TO TAKE THE TIME TO PUT ON SHOES, THESE WOULD BE A GREAT OPTION AS A SLIP-ON FOR THAT SUPPORT.
I LOVE HOW THEY LOOK AND THE FIT THAT THEY HAVE. THE VELCRO ON THE TOP IS ADJUSTABLE FOR COMFORT. THE COLOR I PICKED IS AS GOOD IN PERSON AS ADVERTISED. I HAVE CHOSEN NOT TO TAKE A PICTURE OF ME WEARING THEM SINCE MY TOES ARE SO WONKY (TRUST ME, IT WOULD BE A NEGATIVE VISUAL THAT ONE WOULD NOT GET OUT OF THEIR HEAD AFTER SEEING THEM!)
THEY ARE EASY TO CLEAN IF WORN OUTSIDE OR IF THEY SIMPLY GET DIRTY FROM BEING WORN AROUND THE HOUSE.

ALL THAT BEING SAID, I WANT TO MAKE PROSPECTIVE BUYERS AWARE OF SOMETHING THAT I LEARNED. INITIALLY, THEY WERE HARD TO BREAK IN. THEY FELT STIFF AND ALMOST LIKE WOODEN SHOES WHEN I WALKED IN THEM. BUT GRADUALLY THEY BEGAN TO SOFTEN UP SO I COULD ROLL MY FOOT WHEN WALKING.
AFTER A WHILE, I BEGAN TO HAVE INCREASING DISCOMFORT AND SHARP PAINS IN MY INNER ANKLES. WHEN I HAD A FOLLOW-UP VISIT WITH MY DOCTOR, HE STATED THAT SOMETIMES THE SUPPORTIVE ARCH IS A BIT TOO HIGH FOR PEOPLE WITH MY PROBLEMS. HE SUGGESTED THAT I WEAR THEM ONLY FOR BRIEF TIMES (LIKE MIDDLE-OF-THE NIGHT BATHROOM VISITS) AND USE MY OWN DISCRESSION FOR HOW LONG TO WEAR THEM IF I CHOOSE TO USE THEM FOR OTHER OCCASIONS.

I WOULD HIGHLY RECOMMEND THESE TO ANYONE WITH FLAT FEET. THE PROS LISTED ABOVE ARE REALLY NICE AND I DON'T REGRET PURCHASING THESE AT ALL. I WILL (AND ALREADY HAVE) FOLLOW MY DOCTOR'S SUGGESTIONS AND CONTINUE TO WEAR THEM SINCE THEY ARE SO STINKIN' CUTE. BUT I ALSO WANT TO MAKE OTHERS AWARE OF WHAT I LEARNED SO THAT THEY CAN MAKE AN INFORMED DECISION. HOPEFULLY MY REVIEW WILL HELP OTHERS IF THEY THINK THE SHOE IS CAUSING THEIR CONDITION(S) TO BE MORE COMPLICATED. MODERATION, MY FRIENDS. MODERATION!

top_exclaim.text:
BANANA KINGDOM GIRL'S FLORAL PRINTED HOODIE

This is a colorful hoodie for young girls. This is an XL - or as stated under the XL size, it says 12 years. My grand girl is 11 and wears a size 14 shirt and this DOES NOT fit her. She is tall and has a medium frame. And this is how it fits now, when she tried it on -- NOT after it has been washed. My big concern is that it is 100% cotton -- so we will wash in cold and LINE DRY. I would not dare throw this hoodie in the dryer even on a low setting.

Size issues aside, this is a decent LOOKING little hoodie. It is bright and colorful; the yellow and white designs against the royal blue of the hoodie make for a bright and eye-catching hoodie that looks great with jeans. It is nicely made; the sleeves end with cuffs and so does the bottom. There are two side kangaroo pockets that are deep enough for tissues and lip balm. There are ties through the actual hood but our family is weird, we always remove these! The hood itself is reinforced with extra material so it stays up on your head or will lay down flat.

!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

**HOWEVER -- WHEN SHE TRIED THIS HOODIE ON AND ZIPPED IT UP, THE ZIPPER CAME LOOSE AT THE BOTTOM. ** Now it cannot be zipped up properly without a struggle and may have to be worn open -- BRAND NEW with a broken zipper --

So, it runs very super small and the zipper is pretty much worthless. Careful shopping. So disappointed, as this is a really cute little hoodie!
top_exclaim.title: DO NOT WASTE YOUR MONEY

top_five_star_obscene.text: So many knock offs of crocs that it's easy to confuse them for the real thing. I've picked up the fake ones, even though I've had the real ones. But maybe your old ones wore out and you're at the everything for $5 store and you buy the crappy ones, and you just end up forgetting how great and different real crocs are. If you need extra room in the toe area to use toe separators, or you have wide feet, and/or you have some foot issues with pain, CROCS can be a saver for your feet. When I put mine on today, after having worn the crappy ones for a while, I was excited to have them again as I felt the little nubs inside stimulate my feet. They will help your circulation. I just love them!

Thankfully my Wife is very pleased with this. Gents, do what I did - Be honest. "Hey Honey, this looked so much like our little boy that I had to have it, BUT, it's not actual Pandora".. If you Wife is as cool as mine, she won't care.

In this case the quality exceeds the cost. This is not cheap, it's not "brushed silver" or "plated" or any of those other words they use to describe crap. It's heavy, it's fully silver, it's well detailed, and after 5 weeks it has not shown ANY sign of wear, leaving green marks, turning crappy. None of that. Just high quality, and very cute.
top_five_star_obscene.title: It's adorable and high quality..

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I previously bought a pair of these jeans to replace a retired pair of my Arizona jeans, but they arrived in the wrong size and therefore became capris (which I liked). I decided on getting another pair, but this time chose the "long" length and do not regret it. I am 5'6" and around 140 lbs, and I bought the size 4 which fit me perfectly. The legs are indeed very long, which I anticipate will slowly shrink over the course of many washes down to the perfect length. For now, I just cuff them at the bottom and they're cute and functional. I will likely wear these with some boots most of the winter.
My only qualm with these jeans is that it seems Levi's are becoming more and more cheaply made as the years pass. My first pair were thick, sturdy, and lasted about 3 years; I predict maybe getting a year out of these tops. They seem very thin, not protective at all against brush when I go hiking this fall. The stitching seems solid, only a couple very small errors which shouldn't give me any grief. The deep blue color will probably stain the crap out of my other clothes, considering my phone case has already seen some blue from being in my pocket less than 3 hours, so I will wash them separately for the first few rounds through.
Other than the decreasing quality of what was once a great American brand, I will wear these until they perish. They look and feel nice, so their relatively crappy quality shouldn't be noticeable to anyone but me. If you need some cheap jeans, you've found them; if you want really nice jeans, however, keep looking.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Comfortable and cute, but rather thin material.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Amazing shirt! I have no complaint. I don't know what S, M, L means in dress shirts as I'm a 16-17 in dress shirts in US sizes. However, I went through many reviews and tried to find reviews that mention sizes, specifically chest size in US measurements. Many said to get a size up, that is gibberish to me, as what works for one does not work for all. However, I took a gamble and was not disappointed. The large worked for me with room to spare. I', 5'8" 180# chest 38" for a suit jacket pants 32-34" depending on the make! It's totally complicated to shop nowadays as most companies are doing too much extra crap with their design... skinny, slim fit... etc! I don't care for that extra stuff I just want normal-fitting pants and shirts. is that too much to ask for? ugh! and I'm only 38 years old and still struggling with all this slim crap they have all over!!!!!!

hope my review helps the normal folks that just want a normal-fitting shirt and pants!
top_five_star_obscene.title: Surprised Purchase! Happy Customer!
